content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
September 9,
1993
Melinda Melendez
Principal Consultant
Senate Office of Research
1020
N Street,
Suite 565
Sactamento,
CA
95814
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-93--317
Dear Ms.
Melendez:
This is in reply to your request for confirmation of
telephone advice provided to you on May 17,
1993 regarding your
duties under the Political Reform Act
(the
“Act”) •1
OUESTION
Is it permissible for you to accept an honorarium for which
you contracted prior to your Senate employment but which was to be
paid after you began your Senate employment?
CONCLUSION
It is permissible for you to accept this honorarium.
FACTS
You are a designated employee for the California Senate
Office of Research.
On March 22,
1993, prior to your Senate
employment
(which commenced on April 15,
1993),
you signed a
contract with Kellogg Foundation by which you agreed to attend a
workshop in Battle Creek,
Michigan,
on May
3—5,
1993.
In exchange
for your attendance at the workshop,
the Kellogg Foundation agreed
to pay for your travel expenses related to attending the workshop
and,
in addition,
pay you an honorarium in the sum of $800.
Commission staff member Wayne Imberi advised you by telephone
on May 17,
1993 that it was permissible for you to accept the
honorarium payment,
even though you were employed as a designated
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—93—317
Page
2
employee of the Senate when the payment was to be made.
Apparently, Kellogg Foundation has asked you to provide written
confirmation of this advice from the Commission.
ANALYSIS
Section 89503 prohibits various state officials,
including
state agency designated employees
2 such as you,
from accepting any
honorarium from any source.
Included in the definition of
honorarium is a payment in consideration for attendance at any
public or private conference.
(Section 89502(b).)
Therefore,
had
you contracted for and received payment from the Kellogg
Foundation for attendance at this conference after you became a
Senate employee,
your acceptance of the payment would have been
prohibited.
However,
since the contract occurred prior to your employment
with the Senate,
your receipt of the payment after you began your
employment does not constitute receipt of an illegal honorarium.
Presumably,
one of the purposes for prohibiting certain state
employees from accepting honoraria was to eliminate the appearance
of corruption when they receive payments for activities requiring
little or no work in exchange.
This purpose is not served by
prohibiting contractual arrangements made by the employee prior to
his or her government employment.
This is to confirm,
however, that the value of the travel
payments
(including any hotel accommodations or meals)
provided to
you for this trip,
as well as the honorarium payment itself, is
reportable on your statement of economic interests.
Also,
since
the value of these payments exceeds $250,
you may be required to
disqualify yourself for
12 months from participation in any Senate
decisions that have a material financial effect on the Kellogg
Foundation.
3
Finally,
as Mr.
Imberi informed you, whether you
report the $800 honorarium as “income” or a “gift” on your
statement of economic interests depends on whether you rendered
services that constituted full and adequate consideration for the
payment.
Under Section 82028(a),
any person who claims that a
payment is income and not a gift due to the rendering of full and
adequate consideration has the burden of proving this if the claim
is questioned.
Otherwise,
the payment will be considered a gift
and should be reported as such.
2
For purposes of the Act, the Legislature is a state agency and
employees thereof state agency employees.
(See Section 82049.)
However,
we would need additional facts to determine if,
in
fact, this payment from the Kellogg Foundation actually requires
disqualification.
There are several exceptions that may apply in
this regard.
(See, e.g.,
Section 82030(b) (2)
and Regulation
18704.)


File No.
A—93—317
Page
3
Should you have further questions, please contact me at
(916)
322—5901.
Since ely,
cott Hallabrin
Assistant General Counsel
SH: ak

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Is it permissible for you to accept an honorarium for which
you contracted prior to your Senate employment but which was to be
paid after you began your Senate employment?

CONCLUSION: It is permissible for you to accept this honorarium.

CONCLUSION It is permissible for you to accept this honorarium. FACTS You are a designated employee for the California Senate Office of Research. On March 22, 1993, prior to your Senate employment (which commenced on April 15, 1993), you signed a contract with Kellogg Foundation by which you agreed to attend a workshop in Battle Creek, Michigan, on May 3—5, 1993. In exchange for your attendance at the workshop, the Kellogg Foundation agreed to pay for your travel expenses related to attending the workshop and, in addition, pay you an honorarium in the sum of $800. Apparently, Kellogg Foundation has asked you to provide written confirmation of this advice from the Commission. ANALYSIS Section 89503 prohibits various state officials, including state agency designated employees 2 such as you, from accepting any honorarium from any source. Included in the definition of honorarium is a payment in consideration for attendance at any public or private conference. (Section 89502(b).)
